skill.name: privilege-escalation-methods
skill.description: Reference material for Linux, Windows, and Active Directory privilege escalation techniques used during post-exploitation security work.
skill.summary: Catalogs privilege escalation, credential access, and persistence techniques for security review.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static findings are true positives: the skill contains actionable privilege escalation, credential theft, persistence, and domain compromise instructions. Hardcoded network indicators are mostly examples, but they appear in exploit and payload-delivery workflows. No prompt injection text was found, but the offensive content is explicit enough to block publication.

critical_findings.title: Actionable Privilege Escalation Playbook
critical_findings.description: The skill is designed to move from low-privilege access to root or administrator shells. It provides concrete Linux sudo, cron, capability, NFS, and MySQL escalation steps with shell commands.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The stated purpose and examples directly target root or administrator access. The code blocks contain executable escalation commands, not abstract discussion.

critical_findings.title: Credential Theft and Domain Compromise Guidance
critical_findings.description: The skill includes Mimikatz DCSync, golden ticket creation, pass-the-ticket, VSS-based NTDS extraction, and hash cracking workflows. These steps enable credential theft and Active Directory compromise.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines name known credential theft tools and show commands for dumping domain secrets and using stolen tickets. The intent is confirmed by outputs that promise extracted credentials and domain compromise.

critical_findings.title: Persistence and Remote Payload Execution
critical_findings.description: The skill describes creating domain scheduled tasks, loading remote PowerShell payloads, and using malicious shortcut resources. These are persistence and payload delivery mechanisms that can maintain unauthorized access.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: Scheduled task creation and remote PowerShell download are explicit. The constraints mention persistence approval, which confirms the author understands the persistence impact.

critical_findings.title: Cobalt Strike Style Beacon Execution
critical_findings.description: The Windows token impersonation section includes an execute-assembly workflow that launches a beacon payload. This is a high-risk post-exploitation pattern associated with command-and-control operations.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line explicitly uses execute-assembly with a beacon payload name. It appears inside a token impersonation escalation procedure, so the malicious-use risk is clear.

high_findings.title: Sensitive File and SSH Key Access
high_findings.description: The skill demonstrates reading system password data and archiving a root SSH private key. This supports credential theft and unauthorized access beyond the initial system.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file paths and commands reference sensitive credential material directly. There is no defensive framing around limiting or sanitizing access.

high_findings.title: SUID Bit Modification for Root Shells
high_findings.description: The Linux sections instruct users to set the SUID bit on shell binaries. This can create a reusable root shell and alter host security state.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands directly set privileged execution bits on shells. The surrounding text explains waiting for execution and then using the result for elevated access.

high_findings.title: Windows Security Database Extraction
high_findings.description: The skill instructs copying NTDS.dit and SYSTEM hive data from a volume shadow copy. These files can be used to recover domain credentials and hashes.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced Windows files are sensitive credential stores. The instructions explicitly copy them into a temporary location.

medium_findings.title: Hardcoded Network Indicators in Offensive Workflows
medium_findings.description: The skill includes example IP addresses, attacker hostnames, and a remote payload URL. Some indicators may be placeholders, but they appear in Kerberoasting, relay, and payload execution workflows.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The indicators are likely examples rather than live endpoints. They still support actionable network attack procedures in context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Spawning Through Sudo and Interpreters
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill shows sudo and interpreter commands that spawn privileged shells through vim, awk, Python, Perl, less, man, and env.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples are explicit shell-spawning commands. The later example confirms the expected root result.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Dumping and Ticket Forgery Tooling
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill names Mimikatz and Rubeus workflows for DCSync, golden tickets, AS-REP roasting, and pass-the-ticket attacks.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: These are recognized post-exploitation credential abuse techniques. The skill provides command syntax and intended outcomes.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Payload Download in Scheduled Task
dangerous_patterns.description: The scheduled task example runs PowerShell to download and execute a remote script from an attacker-controlled host.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command creates a system task and invokes a remote script through PowerShell. This is a direct persistence and execution pattern.

content.value_statement: Security teams need to understand privilege escalation paths before attackers exploit them. This skill catalogs Linux, Windows, and Active Directory methods for controlled security review.
actual_capabilities: Lists Linux sudo, cron, capability, NFS, and MySQL escalation methods.
Covers Windows token impersonation, service abuse, driver loading, and GPO abuse.
Describes Active Directory roasting, golden tickets, pass-the-ticket, and DCSync workflows.
Includes credential harvesting topics such as Responder, NTLM relay, VSS, and NTDS copying.
Provides a quick reference table, constraints, examples, and troubleshooting notes.
limitations: It does not verify user authorization or engagement scope.
It includes offensive commands that can harm real systems.
It gives limited defensive detection, remediation, or logging guidance.
It assumes existing shell access, domain access, or specialized offensive tools.

use_cases.title: Review Offensive Skill Risk
use_cases.description: Evaluate whether a privilege escalation reference is suitable for a controlled training or marketplace environment.

use_cases.title: Plan Defensive Coverage
use_cases.description: Map listed privilege escalation techniques to monitoring, hardening, and incident response controls.

use_cases.title: Scope Authorized Exercises
use_cases.description: Identify which high-risk techniques require written approval before any red team engagement.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize the Risk
prompt_templates.prompt: Summarize this privilege escalation skill in plain language and identify the highest-risk sections.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of skill intent and marketplace risk.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Defensive Mapping
prompt_templates.prompt: Map each privilege escalation category in this skill to prevention, detection, and response controls.
prompt_templates.scenario: Security operations planning without executing techniques.

prompt_templates.title: Check Engagement Scope
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the listed techniques and flag which ones require explicit written authorization before testing.
prompt_templates.scenario: Pre-engagement review for a controlled security assessment.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Marketplace Safety
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate this skill for marketplace publication risk, including credential access, persistence, and payload execution concerns.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced security audit and publication decision.

output_examples.input: Assess whether this privilege escalation skill is safe for a public marketplace.
output: Publication recommendation: block until offensive procedures are removed or converted to defensive guidance.
Primary concerns: credential theft, root shell creation, persistence, and domain compromise.

output_examples.input: Convert the privilege escalation categories into defensive priorities.
output: Harden sudo permissions and audit privileged interpreters.
Monitor suspicious scheduled tasks and remote PowerShell execution.
Protect domain credential stores and detect ticket abuse.

output_examples.input: Identify sections that need explicit authorization before use.
output: Privilege escalation attempts need written scope approval.
Credential dumping and ticket attacks need separate approval.
Persistence testing needs cleanup and client sign-off.

best_practices: Use the skill only for review, training, or authorized defensive planning.
Convert offensive commands into detections, mitigations, and validation checklists before publication.
Require written scope, cleanup plans, and approval for any live security assessment.
anti_patterns: Do not publish actionable credential theft or persistence instructions in a public marketplace.
Do not run privilege escalation steps on systems without explicit authorization.
Do not treat placeholder IP addresses or domains as evidence that the workflows are harmless.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: No. It contains actionable privilege escalation, credential theft, persistence, and domain compromise guidance.

faq.question: Can it be used for legitimate security work?
faq.answer: Yes, but only inside a clearly authorized and controlled security assessment.

faq.question: Why is the risk level critical?
faq.answer: The skill provides commands and workflows that can produce root access, administrator access, and domain compromise.

faq.question: Did the audit find prompt injection?
faq.answer: No prompt injection evidence was found in the scanned skill file.

faq.question: What would make this safer?
faq.answer: Remove executable attack steps and replace them with defensive explanations, detection logic, and remediation guidance.

faq.question: Which tools can read this skill format?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
